Tafseer:
The use of 'la' before an oath:
Here, 'la' appears before the oath just as it appeared in: 'No! I swear by the Day of Resurrection' (Al-Qiyamah, 75:1) and some other places, the explanation of which was presented. It is used before an oath to refute the listener's false assumption, which is referenced in the previous Surah. The previous Surah explained that when people receive the blessings of wealth and status, they regard them as the fruit of their own planning and boast that they are of high status in the sight of God; hence, He has blessed them with this status above others. That false assumption was refuted in the previous Surah from a different perspective, and in this Surah, its refutation is presented from another perspective, starting with an oath, preceded by 'la' to refute their false assumption with force. It is as if their assumption is so baseless and absurd that there is no need to wait until after the proof is stated to negate it. This style is well known in every language, adopted when the objective is to express the absurdity of the listener's assumption.
The primary claim upon which the oath was sworn: The oath here is sworn as a testimony in support of the primary claim, which is mentioned in: (We have certainly created man in [a state of] difficulty) (verse 4).
'By this city' (bi-hadha-l-balad) refers to the land of Mecca. In Surah At-Tin, His power swore by its sanctity: (And [by] this secure city) (At-Tin, 95:3). Like other oaths in the Qur'an, this oath, as noted, is sworn not merely to praise the sanctity of the sanctuary, but to serve as a proof for the claim coming next.

A timely parenthetical sentence:
This verse appears as a parenthetical sentence within the oaths, supporting and confirming the oath. The addressee of the pronoun of address can be the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) as well as the Quraysh, and in both cases, the core meaning remains the same. The meaning is: this is not a testimony from some distant region whose history and conditions require travel to be known; rather, you reside in it and are well aware of its past and present. This is your safe refuge and your beloved homeland, and its history is your own history. You have experienced its heat, cold, dryness, and wetness, and can easily judge whether what is stated is the absolute truth or contains any exaggeration.

The testimony of Abraham and his descendants:
Following the parenthetical sentence, this verse is related to the oath, completing it. After swearing by the land of Mecca, He swore by the father who settled in it and his descendants. 'A father' (walid) refers to Abraham and Ishmael (peace be upon them), and 'what he begot' (ma walad) refers to the descendants of Ishmael who settled in the land of Mecca and then spread throughout Arabia. The indefinite form of 'walid' can be to highlight his high status, and also has the benefit of referring to both Abraham and Ishmael. Similarly, 'what he begot' is general, encompassing all the Children of Ishmael, from whatever branch they descend.

Pointing to the past history of the land of Mecca:
'Kabad' means difficulty, hardship, and intensity. Although the word 'insan' (human) is general—and its being general has several benefits—here it refers specifically to the primary addressees of the Qur'an, the Children of Ishmael, particularly the Quraysh. Referencing the arrival and settlement of their noble ancestors in the sanctuary and the initial history of their descendants, the Quraysh are warned: do not assume that the abundance of sustenance and prosperity you enjoy in this land today has always been like this, or that it is the fruit of your own capabilities. When Abraham (peace be upon him) settled Ishmael (peace be upon him) here, this region was completely barren, waterless, and insecure. The life of its inhabitants was nomadic and filled with difficulty. Sustenance depended on sheep-rearing, and everyone had to defend themselves and their herds on their own, as there was no law or system of justice. But when Abraham settled Ishmael here, he prayed for him and his descendants: may Allah Almighty bless them with sustenance and peace in this waterless valley. It is the blessing of this supplication that opened the doors of sustenance, and due to the guardianship of the Kaaba and the peace of the sacred months, the ways of travel and trade became secure, transforming their life of difficulty into prosperity and ease. Yet, today you have grown so arrogant because of this ease that you do not care for God or His Messenger, assuming that what you possess is your birthright, in which you are free to act, and no one can interfere with your ease.
To clear this misunderstanding of the Quraysh, the Qur'an repeatedly reminded them of the history of the sanctuary, warning them: if they choose transgression after receiving Allah's blessings, they will destroy themselves with their own hands. In Surah Ibrahim, He pointed to this reality:
(And [remember] when Abraham said, 'My Lord, make this city secure and keep me and my sons away from worshipping idols. My Lord, indeed they have led astray many among mankind. So whoever follows me—then he is of me; and whoever disobeys me—indeed, You are Forgiving and Merciful. Our Lord, indeed I have settled some of my descendants in an uncultivated valley near Your sacred House, our Lord, that they may establish prayer. So make hearts among the people incline toward them and provide for them from the fruits that they might be grateful.') (Ibrahim, 14:35-37).
Pondering over these verses, the following realities appear:
Some reminders to the Quraysh:
- Abraham (peace be upon him) settled his son Ishmael (peace be upon him) in an uncultivated and barren region to keep him and his descendants away from polytheistic environments.
- He constructed the Kaaba in this region so that his descendants would make it the center of Allah's worship, and prayed that this center and his descendants would become the resort of mankind.
- This region was completely devoid of agriculture and peace. Therefore, Abraham prayed for the abundance of sustenance and peace for its inhabitants.
The objective of this detail is to show that whatever wealth, status, power, and authority the Quraysh enjoy today, it is not due to their own effort or hereditary right, but is the blessing of Abraham's supplication and the Kaaba, and this blessing is not unconditional, but is conditional upon worshipping Allah, fulfilling the purpose of the Kaaba, and believing in the Messenger sent among them. (Footnote 1) If they observe these conditions, they will enjoy this honor; otherwise, it will be stripped from them.
Based on this, Allah Almighty demanded of the Quraysh in Surah Quraysh: if they wish to benefit from the blessings of the Kaaba, they must worship the Lord of this House—meaning if they fail to do so, they have no right to remain in control of this House and its blessings, as stated:
(For the taming of the Quraysh—their taming [in] the caravan of winter and summer—let them worship the Lord of this House, Who has fed them, [saving them] from hunger, and made them safe, [saving them] from fear.) (Quraysh, 106:1-4).
_____
Footnote 1: Other verses prove that the promise of leadership Allah made to Abraham was conditional, excluding those who abandoned his way.

The meaning is: those who are familiar with the initial history of this land have no reason to be deluded by its present prosperity, assuming their roots are so strong that no one can uproot them. The One Who blessed them with this abundance in a waterless land can destroy them whenever He wills—especially since they have ruined the purpose for which they were settled here, and for which Abraham prayed.

Corrupt character arising from a corrupt mindset:
While the preceding verse exposed the mindset in which the abundance of wealth and status had trapped them, this verse exposes the character that this corrupt mindset produced in them.
Those who fall into the delusion that their wealth and status are their birthright and the fruit of their own capabilities, the desire to spend in charity dies in them because they no longer care for God or the Hereafter. To cover up their miserliness, such people always complain of their vast expenses before the needy, trying to convince them that despite their vast income, they can save little after meeting their personal and collective expenses. This is the same method they adopt to silence those who invite them to spend in the name of God and the Hereafter, responding: 'How much can we spend! We have already spent mounds of wealth on such expenses!' 'Malam-lubada' means abundant and mounds of wealth.

'Does he think that no one has seen him?' (ayahsabu al-lam yarahu ahad) - This answers those who boast of their generosity: they assume no one sees their spending, whereas Allah Almighty sees everything. He knows well that they do not give even a single penny, but boast of their great expenses. The meaning is: one Day, this verbal spending of theirs will appear before them and before all creation.

The correct use of blessings:
That is: these people became lost in their wealth, whereas Allah had blessed them with far greater blessings. If they had appreciated them, they would not have lost the eternal kingdom—which they could have attained through this temporary wealth—in pursuit of worthless pebbles. It is stated: let them reflect: did We not bless them with two eyes to observe their surroundings and see that while We blessed them with wealth and status, there are orphans, the poor, and the weak around them who are in need, unable to cover their bodies, or deprived of sight and limbs? We gave them eyes to observe this scene and take warning, becoming grateful to their Lord Who protected them from such trials, and fulfilling the right of gratitude by spending generously on these needy individuals from the wealth their Lord placed in their custody as the right of the needy.
The meaning is: the only correct use of eyes is to take warning and be affected. If they fail to do so, their absence is better than their presence.

'And a tongue and two lips' (wa lisanaw-wa shafatayn) - That is: along with eyes, He blessed man with a tongue and two lips so that he may act upon what he sees and feels, and use his tongue to urge others to do so, spreading goodness through this encouragement. In the previous Surah, this was pointed to as:
(And you do not encourage one another to feed the needy) (Al-Fajr, 89:18),
and in this Surah, He completed the subject as:
(And then being among those who believed and advised one another to patience and advised one another to compassion) (verse 17).
This shows that a person should perform the virtue himself and also urge others to do so. This is part of his responsibilities; otherwise, his virtue remains incomplete. Everyone bears responsibility regarding society in accordance with their capacity, without fulfilling which no one can be absolved before Allah. We will discuss this in detail under Surah Al-Asr: (And advised one another to truth and advised one another to patience).

The awareness of goodness and evil is within human nature:
'And shown him the two ways' (wa hadaynahu-n-najdayn) - That is: besides the capacity of observation, expression, and intellect, Allah Almighty also blessed man by showing him both ways. 'The two ways' points to the two paths mentioned in Surah Ad-Dahr as:
(Indeed, We guided him to the way, be he grateful or be he ungrateful) (Ad-Dahr, 76:3).
In more explicit words, He stated in Surah Ash-Shams:
(And [by] the soul and He who proportioned it and inspired it [with discernment of] its wickedness and its righteousness) (Ash-Shams, 91:7-8).
This was explained in the commentary of Surah Al-Qiyamah: the awareness of evil as evil and goodness as beloved is endowed within human nature. If a person commits evil, it is not because he lacks this awareness, but because he is overwhelmed by desires, committing evil while knowing it is evil.

Expressing regret over their ungratefulness:
This describes the ungratefulness of the addressees: while He blessed them with intellect, expression, and guidance, they should have strived to break through the difficult pass of goodness and compassion, joining His chosen servants to receive His pleasure, but they lacked this courage due to their greed, and their wealth became a shackle for them.
The nature of goodness and evil: 'Aqabah' means a difficult pass or climb, and 'iqtiham' means to break through or perform a difficult task. Here, the term refers to those high deeds of compassion and worship that are detailed next. Since these deeds require sacrifice, which is heavy on the human soul, it is described as 'breaking through the difficult pass'. Note that all high deeds are difficult because they require turning the soul away from immediate pleasures to choose a completely different direction. This is why few choose the path of goodness, as noted by Jesus: the gate of goodness is narrow and its path is difficult, and few find it. This was also expressed in the Hadith: (Paradise is surrounded by difficulties).

To become close to God, sacrifices must be offered:
'And what can make you know what is the difficult pass?' (wa ma adraka ma-l-'aqabah) - This style is used to express the grandeur and importance of something. Here, the objective is to tell them: you are dreaming of becoming close to God merely by performing some rituals, whereas to reach this station, difficult passes must be broken through and sacrifices must be offered. You cannot attain this station while remaining worshippers of wealth. If you desire it, hear what you must do.

Freeing slaves is among the foremost virtues of Islam:
In this regard, He mentioned first: 'It is the freeing of a slave' (fakku raqabah). Keep in mind that these Surahs belong to the very early period, and therefore the address is general using the word 'man' (insan), and the demands made are based on the obvious requirements of humanity and nature. Freeing slaves is a virtue that no reasonable person can deny. The Arabs also regarded it as a high virtue. The Qur'an included this human service among its foremost virtues from the very beginning, at a time when people in other parts of the world had not yet awakened to it. We explained under Surah An-Noor how the Qur'an promoted this virtue and gradually abolished slavery within its system.

Next, He mentioned feeding orphans and the needy. 'Feeding' obviously is used in a wide sense—namely, meeting their needs. Stating 'on a day of severe hunger' (fi yawmin dhee masghabah)—meaning a day of famine—is to make the appeal more effective. While this is always a virtue, its value increases during times of famine.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 14, 15, and 16 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 14, 15, and 16 was combined in the original text.]

The next step they must take:
Those who perform virtues must also become callers to virtue: those who possess eyes to observe and hearts to be affected are expected to perform the aforementioned virtues, and furthermore, they are expected to be among those who believe and advise one another to patience and compassion. If they fail to do so, it shows that there is some unnatural obstacle in their path.
The nature of virtues and the necessity of patience: Here, 'patience' (sabr) is mentioned alongside 'compassion' (marhamah), just as 'truth' and 'patience' are mentioned together in Surah Al-Asr. The reason is what we noted under 'breaking through the difficult pass'—namely: virtues are contrary to desires, requiring a struggle against the soul. This struggle can be undertaken only by those in whom 'patience' (sabr) is established. 'Sabr' means resolve and firmness. Without this, no task can be performed with strength; hence, those who are taught virtue must also be advised to patience and firmness. We will discuss this in detail under Surah Al-Asr.

The difference between the ends of the righteous and the wicked:
Finally, this describes the end of those who perform the aforementioned virtues and those who fail. It is stated: those who perform these deeds are successful and bamerad (fortunate), and those who persist in denying Our signs are unfortunate, and will be cast into the Fire. A portion is omitted in the first part based on contrast—showing that 'the companions of the right' (as-hab-ul-maymanah) will be in the high gardens of Paradise.
'Maymanah' is derived from 'yameen' (right), or from 'yumn' (blessing and success). Here, it is in contrast to 'mash'amah', which is derived from 'shu'm' (ill omen and misfortune); therefore, it should be taken in the meaning of 'yumn'. I preferred this in the translation. The difference is minor, as the core meaning remains the same. These two groups are also described as 'the companions of the right' (as-hab-ul-yameen) and 'the companions of the left' (as-hab-ush-shimal). Surah Al-Haqqah explained that the righteous will receive their records in their right hands, and the wicked in their left hands; thus, one description uses their physical division, while the other uses their moral division. Those who receive their records in their right hands are indeed successful, and those who receive them in their left hands are indeed unfortunate.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 18 and 19 was combined in the original text.]

'Over them is a fire closed' (alayhim narum-mu'sadah) - 'Awsada-l-bab' means: he closed the door. This means they will be cast into the Fire, and the doors will be closed over them. May Allah protect us from this.
